title: Password Security, Hashing & Cryptography Fundamentals
description: Master password hashing, salting, key derivation, and cryptographic operations in Python. Learn to store passwords securely, crack weak hashes, and build authentication systems.
learning_outcomes: Understand why plaintext password storage is catastrophic
Implement MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512 hashing with hashlib
Explain and implement salting to defeat rainbow table attacks
Use bcrypt, scrypt, and PBKDF2 for secure password storage
Build a password strength analyser with entropy calculation
Crack weak password hashes using dictionary attacks
Implement HMAC for message authentication codes
Compare hashing algorithms by speed and security properties
Build a complete secure authentication system in Python
Understand key derivation functions and their cost parameters

explanation_styles.analogy: A hash function is like a meat grinder: you put in a steak and get mince. You can always get the same mince from the same steak, but you cannot reverse the mince back into a steak. That is a one-way function.
explanation_styles.visual: Password 'hello' goes into SHA-256 and comes out as '2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824'. Change one letter to 'Hello' and the entire hash changes completely. That is the avalanche effect.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: To store a password securely: (1) Generate a random salt, (2) Combine salt + password, (3) Hash with slow algorithm (bcrypt/scrypt), (4) Store salt + hash together, (5) To verify: re-hash input with same salt and compare.
explanation_styles.code_trace: hashlib.sha256(b'password').hexdigest() computes SHA-256 of the bytes b'password' and returns the 64-character hex string. Every time you hash b'password', you get the exact same output.
explanation_styles.comparison: MD5 vs SHA-256 vs bcrypt: MD5 is fast (billions/sec) and broken -- never use. SHA-256 is fast and collision-resistant but too fast for passwords. bcrypt is deliberately slow (configurable) -- designed specifically for password storage.

theory.introduction:
Passwords are the front door to every digital system. When that front door is made of cardboard -- plaintext storage, weak hashing, no salting -- attackers walk right through. The history of data breaches is a graveyard of companies that got password storage wrong: LinkedIn (6.5 million SHA-1 hashes leaked), Adobe (153 million passwords in reversible encryption), RockYou (32 million plaintext passwords).

In this course, you will learn how passwords should be stored, why most methods fail, and how to implement truly secure authentication in Python. You will understand hash functions at a deep level, implement salting to defeat precomputed attacks, use purpose-built password hashing algorithms (bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2), build password strength analysers, and even write dictionary attack tools to understand exactly how attackers crack weak passwords.

By the end, you will be able to design and implement a complete secure authentication system that follows current best practices used by banks, tech companies, and security-conscious organisations worldwide.
theory.why_important: Password breaches affect billions of users every year. Understanding hashing, salting, and key derivation is essential for any developer building authentication systems. Even if you never build an auth system yourself, understanding these concepts helps you evaluate security practices, respond to breaches, and make informed decisions about password policies.

key_concepts.explanation: Storing passwords as plaintext means anyone with database access (admins, attackers, backup operators) can read every password. When breached, every account is instantly compromised. Users who reuse passwords (most people) lose access to multiple services. Plaintext storage violates every security standard (OWASP, NIST, GDPR).
key_concepts.ai_hint: Imagine writing all your passwords on a whiteboard in a busy office. That is plaintext storage. Anyone walking by can read them all.

key_concepts.explanation: A hash function takes any input and produces a fixed-size output (the digest). Key properties: (1) Deterministic -- same input always gives same output. (2) One-way -- cannot reverse the hash to find the input. (3) Avalanche effect -- tiny input change causes massive output change. (4) Fixed size -- regardless of input length, output is always the same size (e.g., SHA-256 = 32 bytes = 64 hex chars).
key_concepts.example:
import hashlib

# Same input always gives same hash
h1 = hashlib.sha256(b'password').hexdigest()
h2 = hashlib.sha256(b'password').hexdigest()
print(f'Hash 1: {h1}')
print(f'Hash 2: {h2}')
print(f'Same? {h1 == h2}')

# Avalanche effect: one character changes everything
h3 = hashlib.sha256(b'Password').hexdigest()
print(f'Hash 3: {h3}')
print(f'Totally different? {h1 != h3}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: A hash is like a fingerprint -- every person has a unique one, you can match prints, but you cannot reconstruct a person from their fingerprint.

key_concepts.explanation: MD5 (128-bit) and SHA-1 (160-bit) are fast hash functions that are now considered broken for security. MD5 collisions can be generated in seconds. SHA-1 was broken by Google in 2017 (SHAttered attack). Both are still widely encountered in legacy systems and CTF challenges. Never use them for new password storage.
key_concepts.example:
import hashlib

password = b'hello'
md5 = hashlib.md5(password).hexdigest()
sha1 = hashlib.sha1(password).hexdigest()
sha256 = hashlib.sha256(password).hexdigest()

print(f'MD5    ({len(md5):2d} chars): {md5}')
print(f'SHA-1  ({len(sha1):2d} chars): {sha1}')
print(f'SHA-256({len(sha256):2d} chars): {sha256}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: MD5 and SHA-1 are like old locks that thieves have learned to pick. They still work mechanically, but they no longer stop anyone determined.

key_concepts.concept: SHA-256 and SHA-512 -- Stronger But Still Too Fast
key_concepts.explanation: SHA-256 (256-bit) and SHA-512 (512-bit) are part of the SHA-2 family. They are collision-resistant and widely used for file integrity, digital signatures, and blockchain. However, they are too fast for password hashing -- GPUs can compute billions per second, making brute-force feasible. SHA-256 alone is not sufficient for password storage.
key_concepts.example:
import hashlib

data = b'The quick brown fox'
print(f'SHA-256: {hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()}')
print(f'SHA-512: {hashlib.sha512(data).hexdigest()}')
print(f'SHA-256 length: {len(hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest())} hex chars = 256 bits')
print(f'SHA-512 length: {len(hashlib.sha512(data).hexdigest())} hex chars = 512 bits')
key_concepts.ai_hint: SHA-256 is like a very strong safe, but the combination can be tried billions of times per second. For passwords, you need a safe that takes time to open each attempt.

key_concepts.explanation: A rainbow table is a massive precomputed lookup of hash -> password. Attackers compute hashes for millions of common passwords and store them. When they get a hash, they just look it up. Rainbow tables for MD5 and SHA-1 cover billions of passwords. The defence? Salting -- adding random data before hashing so precomputed tables are useless.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A rainbow table is like a phone book of hashes. If your hash is listed, your password is cracked instantly. Salting makes each hash unique, like giving everyone a different phone book.

key_concepts.explanation: A salt is a random string added to each password before hashing. Each user gets a unique salt stored alongside their hash. This means: (1) Same password produces different hashes for different users. (2) Rainbow tables are useless -- they would need a separate table for every possible salt. (3) Salts do not need to be secret, just unique per user.
key_concepts.example:
import hashlib, os

def hash_with_salt(password):
    salt = os.urandom(16)  # 16 random bytes
    salted = salt + password.encode()
    h = hashlib.sha256(salted).hexdigest()
    return salt.hex(), h

def verify(password, salt_hex, stored_hash):
    salt = bytes.fromhex(salt_hex)
    h = hashlib.sha256(salt + password.encode()).hexdigest()
    return h == stored_hash

salt, hashed = hash_with_salt('MyPassword')
print(f'Salt: {salt}')
print(f'Hash: {hashed}')
print(f'Verify correct: {verify("MyPassword", salt, hashed)}')
print(f'Verify wrong:   {verify("WrongPass", salt, hashed)}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: Salt is like adding a unique secret ingredient to each recipe. Even if two people use the same password, their hashes are completely different because their salts are different.

key_concepts.explanation: bcrypt is specifically designed for password hashing. It is deliberately slow (configurable via work factor/rounds), includes automatic salting, and produces a self-contained string with algorithm, cost, salt, and hash all together. Increasing the work factor by 1 doubles the time, letting you scale with hardware improvements. bcrypt is the recommended choice for most applications.
key_concepts.example:
import bcrypt

password = b'SecurePassword123'

# Hash with bcrypt (automatic salt, work factor 12)
hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password, bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=12))
print(f'bcrypt output: {hashed.decode()}')
print(f'Length: {len(hashed)} chars')

# The output contains: $2b$12$<22-char-salt><31-char-hash>
# Verify
print(f'Correct password: {bcrypt.checkpw(password, hashed)}')
print(f'Wrong password:   {bcrypt.checkpw(b"wrong", hashed)}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: bcrypt is like a deliberately slow lock that takes 0.3 seconds per attempt. Try one password? Fine. Try a billion? That is 10 years.

key_concepts.explanation: PBKDF2 (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2) applies a hash function thousands of times with a salt. The iteration count controls the slowness. NIST recommends at least 600,000 iterations for SHA-256. PBKDF2 is built into Python's hashlib and does not require external libraries. Used in Wi-Fi WPA2, disk encryption, and many applications.
key_concepts.example:
import hashlib, os, time

password = b'MyPassword123'
salt = os.urandom(16)

# PBKDF2 with 100,000 iterations
start = time.time()
dk = hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac('sha256', password, salt, 100000)
elapsed = time.time() - start

print(f'Salt: {salt.hex()}')
print(f'Key:  {dk.hex()}')
print(f'Time: {elapsed*1000:.1f} ms')

# Verify
dk2 = hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac('sha256', password, salt, 100000)
print(f'Match: {dk == dk2}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: PBKDF2 is like asking someone to copy a document by hand 100,000 times. Each copy is quick, but 100,000 copies takes real time. That slowness is the security.

key_concepts.explanation: scrypt is designed to be both CPU-intensive and memory-intensive. Unlike PBKDF2 which mainly uses CPU time, scrypt requires large amounts of RAM, making it expensive to parallelise on GPUs or ASICs. Parameters: N (CPU/memory cost, power of 2), r (block size), p (parallelism). Higher N means more memory and time.
key_concepts.example:
import hashlib, os, time

password = b'SecurePass!'
salt = os.urandom(16)

start = time.time()
dk = hashlib.scrypt(password, salt=salt, n=16384, r=8, p=1, dklen=32)
elapsed = time.time() - start

print(f'scrypt output: {dk.hex()}')
print(f'Time: {elapsed*1000:.1f} ms')

# Verify
dk2 = hashlib.scrypt(password, salt=salt, n=16384, r=8, p=1, dklen=32)
print(f'Match: {dk == dk2}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: scrypt needs both CPU time AND memory. It is like a puzzle that requires a huge desk to solve -- you cannot just hire more workers, each worker needs their own huge desk.

key_concepts.explanation: Password entropy measures unpredictability in bits. Entropy = log2(possible_characters ^ length). A random 8-char lowercase password has log2(26^8) = 37.6 bits. Adding uppercase, digits, and symbols increases the character pool. Passwords below 50 bits entropy are considered weak by modern standards. Password strength meters estimate entropy from character classes, length, and pattern detection.
key_concepts.example:
import math, string

def password_entropy(password):
    pool = 0
    if any(c in string.ascii_lowercase for c in password): pool += 26
    if any(c in string.ascii_uppercase for c in password): pool += 26
    if any(c in string.digits for c in password): pool += 10
    if any(c in string.punctuation for c in password): pool += 32
    entropy = len(password) * math.log2(pool) if pool > 0 else 0
    return entropy

for pwd in ['password', 'Password1', 'P@ssw0rd!', 'Xy7$kL9!mN2#', 'correct horse battery staple']:
    e = password_entropy(pwd)
    strength = 'WEAK' if e < 40 else 'FAIR' if e < 60 else 'STRONG' if e < 80 else 'VERY STRONG'
    print(f'{pwd:30s} -> {e:5.1f} bits ({strength})')
key_concepts.ai_hint: Entropy measures how many guesses an attacker needs. Each extra bit doubles the guesses. 40 bits = 1 trillion guesses. 80 bits = a trillion trillion.

key_concepts.explanation: Dictionary attacks try common passwords (from breach lists) against stolen hashes. The attacker hashes each dictionary word and compares it to the target hash. Combined with rules (capitalisation, leet speak, appending numbers), dictionary attacks crack 70-80% of real-world passwords. Understanding attacks helps you build better defences.
key_concepts.example:
import hashlib

def dictionary_attack(target_hash, wordlist):
    for word in wordlist:
        if hashlib.md5(word.encode()).hexdigest() == target_hash:
            return word
    return None

# Target: MD5 hash of an unknown password
target = hashlib.md5(b'sunshine').hexdigest()
print(f'Target hash: {target}')

wordlist = ['password', '123456', 'qwerty', 'letmein', 'sunshine', 'monkey']
result = dictionary_attack(target, wordlist)
print(f'Cracked: {result}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: A dictionary attack is like trying every key on a locksmith's board. Most people use common passwords, so most locks open within minutes.

key_concepts.explanation: HMAC combines a hash function with a secret key to create a message authentication code. Unlike plain hashing, HMAC proves both integrity (message was not changed) and authenticity (message came from someone with the key). Used in API authentication, JWT tokens, and secure communication protocols.
key_concepts.example:
import hmac, hashlib

key = b'super_secret_key'
message = b'Transfer 100 to account 12345'

# Create HMAC
mac = hmac.new(key, message, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
print(f'HMAC: {mac}')

# Verify (constant-time comparison)
received_mac = mac
is_valid = hmac.compare_digest(
    hmac.new(key, message, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest(),
    received_mac
)
print(f'Valid: {is_valid}')

# Tampered message
tampered = b'Transfer 10000 to account 12345'
tampered_check = hmac.compare_digest(
    hmac.new(key, tampered, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest(),
    received_mac
)
print(f'Tampered valid: {tampered_check}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: HMAC is like a wax seal on a letter. If the seal is intact and matches the sender's stamp, you know the letter is genuine and untampered.

key_concepts.explanation: A complete auth system needs: (1) Registration -- hash password with bcrypt/scrypt, store hash. (2) Login -- retrieve hash, verify with same algorithm. (3) Password reset -- generate secure token, expire after use. (4) Rate limiting -- prevent brute force. (5) Constant-time comparison -- prevent timing attacks. Never implement your own crypto; use established libraries.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A secure auth system has many layers: hashing, salting, rate limiting, constant-time comparison. Each layer stops a different type of attack.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Demonstrate the Avalanche Effect
complete_examples.description: Show how one character change completely transforms the hash
complete_examples.code:
import hashlib

def show_avalanche(s1, s2):
    h1 = hashlib.sha256(s1.encode()).hexdigest()
    h2 = hashlib.sha256(s2.encode()).hexdigest()
    diff = sum(1 for a,b in zip(h1,h2) if a!=b)
    print(f'Input 1: "{s1}"')
    print(f'Hash 1:  {h1}')
    print(f'Input 2: "{s2}"')
    print(f'Hash 2:  {h2}')
    print(f'Characters different: {diff}/{len(h1)} ({diff/len(h1)*100:.0f}%)')
    print()

show_avalanche('password', 'Password')
show_avalanche('hello', 'hellp')
show_avalanche('test', 'test!')
complete_examples.output:
Input 1: "password"
Hash 1:  5e884898da28047151d0e56f8dc6292773603d0d6aabbdd62a11ef721d1542d8
Input 2: "Password"
Hash 2:  e7cf3ef4f17c3999a94f2c6f612e8a888e5b1026878e4f982fc1c0c0b89bfb6c
Characters different: 60/64 (94%)
...
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The avalanche effect means a 1-bit input change affects ~50% of output bits. This makes it impossible to guess the input by looking at how close two hashes are.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Implement Password Salting
complete_examples.description: Add a unique salt to each password before hashing
complete_examples.code:
import hashlib, os

def create_salted_hash(password):
    salt = os.urandom(16)
    combined = salt + password.encode()
    h = hashlib.sha256(combined).hexdigest()
    return salt.hex() + ':' + h

def verify_salted_hash(password, stored):
    salt_hex, stored_hash = stored.split(':')
    salt = bytes.fromhex(salt_hex)
    h = hashlib.sha256(salt + password.encode()).hexdigest()
    return h == stored_hash

# Same password, different salts = different hashes
for i in range(3):
    stored = create_salted_hash('SamePassword')
    print(f'Stored: {stored[:40]}...')

stored = create_salted_hash('TestPassword')
print(f'\nVerify correct: {verify_salted_hash("TestPassword", stored)}')
print(f'Verify wrong:   {verify_salted_hash("WrongPassword", stored)}')
complete_examples.output:
Stored: a1b2c3d4...:5e884898da28047151d0e56f...
Stored: e5f6a7b8...:9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0...
Stored: 1234abcd...:ef92b778bafe771e89245b89...

Verify correct: True
Verify wrong:   False
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Each call to create_salted_hash generates a new random salt, so the same password produces different hashes. The salt is stored alongside the hash (salt:hash format). To verify, we extract the salt and re-hash.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Store multiple users and demonstrate that two users with the same password have different hashes.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: bcrypt Password Hashing
complete_examples.description: Use bcrypt for secure password storage with automatic salting
complete_examples.code:
import bcrypt, time

def bcrypt_demo(password, rounds=12):
    pwd = password.encode()
    start = time.time()
    hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(pwd, bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=rounds))
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    
    print(f'Password: {password}')
    print(f'Rounds: {rounds}')
    print(f'Hash: {hashed.decode()}')
    print(f'Time: {elapsed*1000:.0f} ms')
    print(f'Verify: {bcrypt.checkpw(pwd, hashed)}')
    # Breakdown: $2b$12$<salt22chars><hash31chars>
    parts = hashed.decode().split('$')
    print(f'Algorithm: {parts[1]}, Cost: {parts[2]}')
    print()

bcrypt_demo('SecurePass123', 10)
bcrypt_demo('SecurePass123', 12)
complete_examples.output:
Password: SecurePass123
Rounds: 10
Hash: $2b$10$...
Time: 87 ms
Verify: True
Algorithm: 2b, Cost: 10

Password: SecurePass123
Rounds: 12
Hash: $2b$12$...
Time: 340 ms
Verify: True
Algorithm: 2b, Cost: 12
complete_examples.ai_explanation: bcrypt includes the algorithm ($2b$), cost factor (12), salt (22 chars), and hash (31 chars) in one string. Increasing cost by 1 doubles the time. Cost 12 takes ~340ms per hash.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: PBKDF2 Key Derivation
complete_examples.description: Use PBKDF2 with configurable iterations for password hashing
complete_examples.code:
import hashlib, os, time

def pbkdf2_hash(password, iterations=100000):
    salt = os.urandom(16)
    start = time.time()
    dk = hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac('sha256', password.encode(), salt, iterations)
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    return {
        'salt': salt.hex(),
        'hash': dk.hex(),
        'iterations': iterations,
        'time_ms': elapsed * 1000
    }

def pbkdf2_verify(password, salt_hex, stored_hash, iterations):
    salt = bytes.fromhex(salt_hex)
    dk = hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac('sha256', password.encode(), salt, iterations)
    return dk.hex() == stored_hash

result = pbkdf2_hash('MyPassword!', 100000)
print(f'Salt: {result["salt"]}')
print(f'Hash: {result["hash"]}')
print(f'Iterations: {result["iterations"]}')
print(f'Time: {result["time_ms"]:.1f} ms')
print(f'Verify: {pbkdf2_verify("MyPassword!", result["salt"], result["hash"], result["iterations"])}')
complete_examples.output:
Salt: a1b2c3d4e5f6...
Hash: 7f3b5e4d...
Iterations: 100000
Time: 85.2 ms
Verify: True
complete_examples.ai_explanation: PBKDF2 applies HMAC-SHA256 100,000 times. Each iteration feeds the previous output back in. The salt prevents rainbow tables and the high iteration count prevents brute-force.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Benchmark 10,000 vs 100,000 vs 1,000,000 iterations and compare times.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Dictionary Attack Cracker
complete_examples.description: Crack MD5 hashes using a dictionary wordlist
complete_examples.code:
import hashlib, time

def dictionary_attack(target_hashes, wordlist):
    cracked = {}
    start = time.time()
    attempts = 0
    
    for word in wordlist:
        h = hashlib.md5(word.encode()).hexdigest()
        attempts += 1
        if h in target_hashes:
            cracked[h] = word
    
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    print(f'Attempts: {attempts:,} in {elapsed*1000:.1f}ms')
    print(f'Rate: {attempts/elapsed:,.0f} hashes/sec')
    print(f'Cracked: {len(cracked)}/{len(target_hashes)}')
    for h, pwd in cracked.items():
        print(f'  {h} -> {pwd}')
    return cracked

targets = set()
passwords = ['sunshine', 'dragon', 'master', 'Xy7kL9mN']
for p in passwords:
    targets.add(hashlib.md5(p.encode()).hexdigest())

wordlist = ['password','123456','qwerty','letmein','dragon','sunshine','master','monkey','shadow','ashley']
dictionary_attack(targets, wordlist)
complete_examples.output:
Attempts: 10 in 0.1ms
Rate: 100,000 hashes/sec
Cracked: 3/4
  ...-> sunshine
  ...-> dragon
  ...-> master
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Dictionary attacks try known common passwords. 3 of 4 cracked because they were in the wordlist. 'Xy7kL9mN' survived because it was not a dictionary word. This shows why random passwords matter.

complete_examples.title: Example 9: HMAC Message Authentication
complete_examples.description: Create and verify HMACs for message integrity
complete_examples.code:
import hmac, hashlib

def create_hmac(key, message):
    return hmac.new(key.encode(), message.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()

def verify_hmac(key, message, received_mac):
    expected = hmac.new(key.encode(), message.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    return hmac.compare_digest(expected, received_mac)

key = 'api_secret_key_12345'
message = 'user=alice&action=transfer&amount=100'

mac = create_hmac(key, message)
print(f'Message: {message}')
print(f'HMAC:    {mac}')
print(f'Valid:   {verify_hmac(key, message, mac)}')

# Tampered message
tampered = 'user=alice&action=transfer&amount=10000'
print(f'\nTampered: {tampered}')
print(f'Valid:    {verify_hmac(key, tampered, mac)}')
complete_examples.output:
Message: user=alice&action=transfer&amount=100
HMAC:    a1b2c3d4...
Valid:   True

Tampered: user=alice&action=transfer&amount=10000
Valid:    False
complete_examples.ai_explanation: HMAC proves both integrity and authenticity. compare_digest uses constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks. The tampered message fails because changing even one character changes the HMAC.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Hash Speed Benchmark
complete_examples.description: Compare hashing speeds to understand why fast hashes are bad for passwords
complete_examples.code:
import hashlib, time

def benchmark(algo, data=b'password123', iterations=100000):
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(iterations):
        hashlib.new(algo, data).digest()
    elapsed = time.time() - start
    rate = iterations / elapsed
    return elapsed, rate

print(f'{"Algorithm":10s} {"Time (100K)":>12s} {"Rate":>15s}')
print('-' * 40)
for algo in ['md5', 'sha1', 'sha256', 'sha512', 'sha3_256', 'blake2b']:
    elapsed, rate = benchmark(algo)
    print(f'{algo:10s} {elapsed*1000:8.1f} ms   {rate:>12,.0f}/sec')

# Now compare with PBKDF2
start = time.time()
for _ in range(100):
    hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac('sha256', b'password123', b'salt', 100000)
pbkdf2_time = time.time() - start
print(f'{"pbkdf2":10s} {pbkdf2_time*1000:8.1f} ms   {100/pbkdf2_time:>12,.0f}/sec (100 iterations)')
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Fast hashes (MD5 at 2M/sec) let attackers try billions of passwords quickly. PBKDF2 at ~12/sec means each guess takes 80ms. That is the entire point of slow hashing for passwords.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add bcrypt to the benchmark and compare with GPU-estimated speeds.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Secure Password Generator
complete_examples.code:
import secrets, string

def generate_password(length=16, use_upper=True, use_digits=True, use_symbols=True):
    chars = string.ascii_lowercase
    required = [secrets.choice(string.ascii_lowercase)]
    if use_upper:
        chars += string.ascii_uppercase
        required.append(secrets.choice(string.ascii_uppercase))
    if use_digits:
        chars += string.digits
        required.append(secrets.choice(string.digits))
    if use_symbols:
        safe_symbols = '!@#$%^&*-_=+'
        chars += safe_symbols
        required.append(secrets.choice(safe_symbols))
    
    remaining = [secrets.choice(chars) for _ in range(length - len(required))]
    password = required + remaining
    # Shuffle using Fisher-Yates with secure random
    for i in range(len(password)-1, 0, -1):
        j = secrets.randbelow(i + 1)
        password[i], password[j] = password[j], password[i]
    return ''.join(password)

for _ in range(5):
    pwd = generate_password(16)
    print(f'{pwd}  (length: {len(pwd)})')
complete_examples.ai_explanation: secrets module uses OS-level CSPRNG (cryptographically secure pseudo-random number generator). We guarantee at least one character from each class, then shuffle securely. Never use random module for security!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a passphrase generator using random words (XKCD style: 'correct horse battery staple').

complete_examples.title: Example 13: File Integrity Checker with SHA-256
complete_examples.description: Hash files to detect tampering or corruption
complete_examples.code:
import hashlib, os

def hash_file(filepath, algorithm='sha256'):
    h = hashlib.new(algorithm)
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        while chunk := f.read(8192):
            h.update(chunk)
    return h.hexdigest()

def verify_integrity(filepath, expected_hash, algorithm='sha256'):
    actual = hash_file(filepath, algorithm)
    match = actual == expected_hash
    return match, actual

# Create test files
with open('/tmp/test_file.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('This is the original content.')

original_hash = hash_file('/tmp/test_file.txt')
print(f'Original SHA-256: {original_hash}')

# Verify unchanged
match, actual = verify_integrity('/tmp/test_file.txt', original_hash)
print(f'Integrity check: {"PASS" if match else "FAIL"}')

# Tamper with file
with open('/tmp/test_file.txt', 'a') as f:
    f.write('.')
match, actual = verify_integrity('/tmp/test_file.txt', original_hash)
print(f'After tampering: {"PASS" if match else "FAIL"}')
print(f'Hash changed to: {actual}')
complete_examples.output:
Original SHA-256: 7f83b1657ff1fc53b92dc18148a1d65dfc2d4b1fa3d677284addd200126d9069
Integrity check: PASS
After tampering: FAIL
Hash changed to: ...
complete_examples.ai_explanation: File hashing reads data in chunks (memory-efficient) and produces a digest. Any change to the file changes the hash. This is how package managers, forensic tools, and download verification work.

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Complete Secure Authentication System
complete_examples.description: Full registration, login, and password change system
complete_examples.code:
import hashlib, os, hmac as hmac_mod, time, secrets

class AuthSystem:
    def __init__(self, iterations=100000):
        self.users = {}
        self.iterations = iterations
        self.login_attempts = {}
    
    def _hash(self, password, salt=None):
        if salt is None: salt = os.urandom(32)
        dk = hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac('sha256', password.encode(), salt, self.iterations)
        return salt, dk
    
    def register(self, username, password):
        if username in self.users: return False, 'Username taken'
        if len(password) < 8: return False, 'Password too short'
        salt, hashed = self._hash(password)
        self.users[username] = {'salt': salt, 'hash': hashed, 'created': time.time()}
        return True, 'Account created'
    
    def login(self, username, password):
        # Rate limiting
        key = username
        now = time.time()
        if key in self.login_attempts:
            attempts, last = self.login_attempts[key]
            if attempts >= 5 and now - last < 300:
                return False, f'Account locked for {300-(now-last):.0f}s'
        
        if username not in self.users:
            self._hash(password)  # Constant time even for invalid users
            return False, 'Invalid credentials'
        
        user = self.users[username]
        _, check = self._hash(password, user['salt'])
        if hmac_mod.compare_digest(check, user['hash']):
            self.login_attempts.pop(key, None)
            return True, 'Login successful'
        
        attempts = self.login_attempts.get(key, (0, now))
        self.login_attempts[key] = (attempts[0]+1, now)
        return False, 'Invalid credentials'
    
    def change_password(self, username, old_pw, new_pw):
        ok, msg = self.login(username, old_pw)
        if not ok: return False, 'Current password incorrect'
        salt, hashed = self._hash(new_pw)
        self.users[username]['salt'] = salt
        self.users[username]['hash'] = hashed
        return True, 'Password changed'

auth = AuthSystem(iterations=10000)
print(auth.register('alice', 'Str0ng!Pass'))
print(auth.register('bob', 'short'))
print(auth.login('alice', 'Str0ng!Pass'))
print(auth.login('alice', 'wrong'))
print(auth.change_password('alice', 'Str0ng!Pass', 'NewStr0ng!Pass'))
print(auth.login('alice', 'NewStr0ng!Pass'))
complete_examples.output:
(True, 'Account created')
(False, 'Password too short')
(True, 'Login successful')
(False, 'Invalid credentials')
(True, 'Password changed')
(True, 'Login successful')
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Complete auth system with: PBKDF2 hashing, random salts, rate limiting (5 attempts then 5-min lockout), constant-time comparison, timing attack prevention (hash even for invalid users), and password change with old password verification.

visual_diagram.title: Password Security & Hashing — Complete Reference Flowchart
visual_diagram.content:
🔐 Password Security — Complete Reference

📊 Hash Algorithm Comparison

  Algorithm    Output   Speed         Password Use?   Status
  ─────────   ──────   ─────         ─────────────   ──────
  MD5          128-bit  Very Fast     NEVER           Broken (collisions)
  SHA-1        160-bit  Fast          NEVER           Broken (SHAttered)
  SHA-256      256-bit  Fast          Only with PBKDF2  Secure for integrity
  SHA-512      512-bit  Fast          Only with PBKDF2  Secure for integrity
  bcrypt       184-bit  Slow (tunable) YES ✓          Gold standard
  scrypt       Variable Slow+Memory   YES ✓          Memory-hard
  PBKDF2       Variable Slow (tunable) YES ✓         NIST recommended
  Argon2       Variable Slow+Memory   YES ✓ (best)   PHC winner

🔄 Password Storage Workflow

  REGISTRATION
     │
     ▼
  User enters password ─── "MyP@ssw0rd!"
     │
     ▼
  Generate random salt ─── os.urandom(16) → a7b3c9d1e5f2...
     │
     ▼
  Hash with slow algorithm
     │   bcrypt.hashpw(password, gensalt(rounds=12))
     │   OR hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac('sha256', pw, salt, 100000)
     ▼
  Store in database
     │   username | salt | hashed_password
     │   alice    | a7b3 | $2b$12$...
     ▼
  ✅ Done! Password is never stored as plaintext.


  LOGIN VERIFICATION
     │
     ▼
  User enters password ─── "MyP@ssw0rd!"
     │
     ▼
  Retrieve salt from DB ─── salt = a7b3c9d1e5f2...
     │
     ▼
  Hash input with SAME salt + algorithm
     │
     ▼
  Compare (constant-time!)
     │
     ├── Match ──→ ✅ Login granted
     └── No match → ❌ Invalid credentials

⚡ Attack Types vs Defences

  Attack               How It Works                    Defence
  ──────               ────────────                    ───────
  Plaintext theft      Read DB directly                Hash passwords
  Rainbow table        Precomputed hash lookup         Salt each password
  Brute force          Try every combination           Slow hash (bcrypt)
  Dictionary attack    Try common passwords            Slow hash + policy
  GPU cracking         Parallel hash computation       Memory-hard (scrypt)
  Timing attack        Measure comparison time         hmac.compare_digest
  Credential stuffing  Reuse leaked passwords          Unique salts + MFA
  Pass-the-hash        Use stolen hash directly        bcrypt (not reversible)

🔑 Python hashlib Quick Reference

  Basic hashing:
  hashlib.sha256(b'data').hexdigest()           # One-shot
  h = hashlib.sha256(); h.update(b'data'); h.hexdigest()  # Streaming

  PBKDF2 (password hashing):
  hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac('sha256', password, salt, iterations)
  # Returns bytes. iterations >= 100,000 recommended

  scrypt (memory-hard):
  hashlib.scrypt(password, salt=salt, n=16384, r=8, p=1, dklen=32)
  # n = CPU/memory cost (power of 2), r = block size, p = parallelism

  HMAC (message auth):
  import hmac
  hmac.new(key, message, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
  hmac.compare_digest(a, b)  # Constant-time comparison!

  bcrypt (external library):
  import bcrypt
  hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password, bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=12))
  bcrypt.checkpw(password, hashed)  # Returns True/False

  Secure random:
  import secrets, os
  os.urandom(16)              # 16 random bytes (salt)
  secrets.token_hex(32)       # 32 hex chars
  secrets.token_urlsafe(32)   # URL-safe base64 token

📏 Password Entropy Guide

  Entropy = length × log₂(pool_size)

  Character Pool Sizes:
    lowercase only (a-z)         = 26
    + uppercase (A-Z)            = 52
    + digits (0-9)               = 62
    + symbols (!@#$...)          = 94

  Strength Levels:
    < 30 bits   VERY WEAK     "pass" (4 chars, lowercase)
    30-44 bits  WEAK          "password" (8 chars, lowercase)
    45-59 bits  FAIR          "Password1" (9 chars, mixed)
    60-79 bits  STRONG        "P@ssw0rd!x" (10 chars, all classes)
    80+ bits    VERY STRONG   "Xy7$kL9!mN2#qR" (14 chars, all classes)

  Cracking Time (at 10 billion guesses/sec):
    40 bits  = 1.8 minutes
    50 bits  = 31 hours
    60 bits  = 36 years
    70 bits  = 37,000 years
    80 bits  = 38 million years

🏆 10 Golden Rules of Password Security

  1. NEVER store passwords as plaintext or reversible encryption
  2. Use bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2, or Argon2 — NEVER plain MD5/SHA
  3. Generate a unique random salt for every password
  4. Use hmac.compare_digest() for constant-time comparison
  5. Set bcrypt rounds ≥ 12 or PBKDF2 iterations ≥ 100,000
  6. Use secrets module, not random, for security operations
  7. Enforce minimum password length (12+ characters recommended)
  8. Implement rate limiting and account lockout
  9. Never log or display passwords, even in error messages
  10. Hash even for invalid usernames to prevent user enumeration

Linux crypt: $1$=MD5, $5$=SHA-256, $6$=SHA-512.

def detect_format(h):
    if h.startswith('$2b$') or h.startswith('$2a$'): return 'bcrypt'
    if h.startswith('pbkdf2:'): return 'PBKDF2-SHA256'
    if h.startswith('$6$'): return 'SHA-512 Crypt'
    if h.startswith('$5$'): return 'SHA-256 Crypt'
    if h.startswith('$1$'): return 'MD5 Crypt'
    if len(h)==32: return 'MD5'
    if len(h)==40: return 'SHA-1'
    if len(h)==64: return 'SHA-256'
    return 'Unknown'

import math, string
def calc_entropy(pwd):
    pool = 0
    if any(c in string.ascii_lowercase for c in pwd): pool += 26
    if any(c in string.ascii_uppercase for c in pwd): pool += 26
    if any(c in string.digits for c in pwd): pool += 10
    if any(c in string.punctuation for c in pwd): pool += 32
    return len(pwd) * math.log2(pool) if pool else 0

import math
def estimate_crack_time(length, pool, rate=1e10):
    combos = pool ** length
    secs = combos / rate
    if secs < 60: t = f'{secs:.1f} seconds'
    elif secs < 3600: t = f'{secs/3600:.1f} hours'
    elif secs < 86400*365: t = f'{secs/86400:.0f} days'
    else: t = f'{secs/86400/365:.1e} years'
    print(f'{length} chars (pool {pool}): {combos:.3g} combos = {t}')

practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let us work on password similarity checker! This is a intermediate exercise in Secure Password Generation.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
def too_similar(old_pw, new_pw, threshold=0.6):
    # TODO: Calculate character overlap ratio
    pass

print(too_similar('Password1!', 'Password2!'))  # Very similar
print(too_similar('Password1!', 'Xy7$kL9!mN'))  # Very different

hints.message: Count matching characters at same positions. Ratio = matches / max(len_old, len_new).
Or use set intersection of character bigrams for fuzzy matching.

def too_similar(old_pw, new_pw, threshold=0.6):
    maxlen = max(len(old_pw), len(new_pw))
    matches = sum(1 for a,b in zip(old_pw, new_pw) if a==b)
    sim = matches / maxlen if maxlen else 0
    result = sim >= threshold
    print(f'{result} (similarity: {sim:.2f})')
    return result

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Entropy-Based Password Scorer
practice_exercises.description: Score password 0-100 based on entropy and pattern analysis.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let us work on entropy-based password scorer! This is a intermediate exercise in Secure Password Generation.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
abc                  -> Score: 12/100
password             -> Score: 25/100
P@ssw0rd!            -> Score: 62/100
Xy7$kL9!mN2#qRfT     -> Score: 95/100

hints.message: Base score from entropy (0-50 points). Bonus for length >12 (+20), all classes (+20), no patterns (+10).
Penalty for common words, sequences, repeats.

import math, string, re
def score_password(pwd):
    pool = 0
    if re.search(r'[a-z]',pwd): pool+=26
    if re.search(r'[A-Z]',pwd): pool+=26
    if re.search(r'[0-9]',pwd): pool+=10
    if re.search(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9]',pwd): pool+=32
    entropy = len(pwd)*math.log2(pool) if pool else 0
    score = min(50, int(entropy*50/80))
    if len(pwd)>=12: score+=20
    if pool>=62: score+=15
    if pool>=94: score+=5
    if re.search(r'(.)\1{2,}',pwd): score-=10
    if pwd.lower() in ['password','123456','qwerty']: score=max(5,score-30)
    return max(0, min(100, score))

hints.message: SHA-1 hash the password and check if it is in the breach set.
This is how haveibeenpwned API works (k-anonymity uses first 5 chars of SHA-1).

import hashlib
def check_breach(password, breach_hashes):
    h = hashlib.sha1(password.encode()).hexdigest()
    return 'BREACHED!' if h in breach_hashes else 'Safe'

hints.message: Password entropy: length * math.log2(charset_size). Passphrase: num_words * math.log2(wordlist_size).
Determine character pool by checking for lowercase(26), uppercase(26), digits(10), symbols(32).

import math, re
def compare_strength(password, num_words, wordlist_size=7776):
    pool = 0
    if re.search(r"[a-z]",password): pool+=26
    if re.search(r"[A-Z]",password): pool+=26
    if re.search(r"[0-9]",password): pool+=10
    if re.search(r"[^a-zA-Z0-9]",password): pool+=32
    pw_ent = len(password)*math.log2(pool) if pool else 0
    pp_ent = num_words*math.log2(wordlist_size)
    print(f"Password ({len(password)} chars, pool={pool}): {pw_ent:.1f} bits")
    print(f"Passphrase ({num_words} words, pool={wordlist_size}): {pp_ent:.1f} bits")
    if abs(pw_ent-pp_ent)<5: print("Similar strength but passphrase is easier to remember!")
    elif pw_ent>pp_ent: print("Password is stronger at this length.")
    else: print("Passphrase is stronger!")
